unit.name: From early machine translation to statistical MT
unit.description: Reviews the history of machine translation and explains the statistical phrase-based decomposition that preceded neural MT.
unit.summary: The machine-translation section starts with historical context: MT was one of the earliest NLP goals in the 1950s, inspired partly by code-breaking and Cold War interest, but it failed because linguistic theory and computing power were both immature [ts=3351s] [ts=3483s]. The lecture then describes the 1990s–2000s shift to statistical machine translation built from large parallel corpora such as EU and UN text [ts=3525s] [ts=3586s]. The core decomposition models translation with Bayes’ rule, treating target-language fluency as a language-model problem while using a simpler translation model for word/phrase correspondences [ts=3607s] [ts=3665s]. A Chinese example from a translated Jared Diamond sentence is used to show how phrase-based systems often fail on long-range modification and reordering, producing fluent fragments but incorrect attachment structure [ts=3736s] [ts=3849s].

key_points.text: Early machine translation in the 1950s was heavily funded but failed because linguistic knowledge and compute were inadequate.
Statistical machine translation used large parallel corpora collected from multilingual sources such as the EU and UN.
Phrase-based MT decomposed translation with Bayes' rule into a translation model and a target-language model.
The translation model handled likely word/phrase correspondences, while the language model handled target-language fluency.
Phrase-based MT often failed to preserve modification relationships and word-order structure, as shown by the Chinese example.

The core decomposition models translation with Bayes’ rule, treating target-language fluency as a language-model problem while using a simpler translation model for word/phrase correspondences [ts=3607s] [ts=3665s]. A Chinese example from a translated Jared Diamond sentence is used to show how phrase-based systems often fail on long-range modification and reordering, producing fluent fragments but incorrect attachment structure [ts=3736s] [ts=3849s].

kp_catalog.name: Statistical machine translation
kp_catalog.description: Pre-neural MT relied on parallel data, Bayes-style factorization, and phrase-based models that often struggled with reordering and attachment.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because Statistical machine translation captures the core idea that Pre-neural MT relied on parallel data, Bayes-style factorization, and phrase-based models that often struggled with reordering and attachment.
